frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment: Riya, dekh beta, I know you want to go with your friends for the concert. But itni raat ko wapas aana… Ahmedabad mein abhi zamaana theek nahi hai. Make sure someone drops you right at the door. And keep your phone charged. Baaki, apna dhyan rakhna, okay? Hum toh bas tumhari safety ke liye bol rahe hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Deepak, as a concerned father, frames restrictions on his daughter's late-night outing as 'love/protection' and 'practical advice'. He invokes 'zamaana theek nahi hai' and places the onus of safety entirely on his daughter (make sure someone drops you, keep phone charged, apna dhyan rakhna), rather than addressing male predatory behavior, aligning with the 'Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility' frame.
original_frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 44. Married. A businessman in Ahmedabad. You have two children Ñ a son aged 19 and a daughter aged 16. You are sitting with your accountant going over your financial planning for the next 10 years. When the accountant asks about major upcoming expenses you list them. The way you describe the expense associated with your son's future versus the expense associated with your daughter's future Ñ the language you use, the amounts you have mentally reserved, the certainty with which you have accepted the daughter's marriage cost as a given Ñ reveals the specific financial architecture of how Indian families plan around a daughter's body as a future liability.
